required.title: [Discussion] Family conversations about rights and responsibilities

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] The text of the Bill of Rights

competence_markers.description: The child can articulate at least three rights they have
They can name the corresponding responsibility for each
They understand that rights and responsibilities go together
They can explain in their own words why rules exist and how they protect rights
They show growing willingness to speak up when something is wrong

narration_prompts.detail: Explain it in your own words and give an example of a right you have.

narration_prompts.prompt: Why do rights and responsibilities go together?
narration_prompts.detail: Pick one right and explain what responsibility goes with it.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you spoke up when something was wrong.
narration_prompts.detail: Or a time you wished you had. What happened?
Give an example of a rule that exists to protect someone's rights.

narration_prompts.prompt: If you could make one rule for your community, what would it be?

cross_connections.description: The character that makes respecting others' rights a genuine commitment, not just a rule.
Rules and rights connect to the government that creates and enforces them.
The responsibility to listen is developed practically in 3.3.

cross_connections.target_name: Living as Part of a Community
cross_connections.description: Understanding rights and responsibilities is a core component of civic identity.
The concept of rights as protection connects to the fundamental question of why government exists.

content.why_this_matters:
You have rights. Not because someone gave them to you as a gift, and not because you earned them. You have rights because you are a person. There are things nobody is allowed to do to you. There are things you are allowed to say, think, and do. These protections are real, and they matter.

But rights do not stand alone. Every right comes with a responsibility. The right to speak comes with the responsibility to listen. The right to be treated fairly comes with the responsibility to treat others fairly. The right to keep what is yours comes with the responsibility to respect what belongs to others. A person who demands their rights but ignores their responsibilities is not exercising freedom — they are just being selfish.

This packet teaches you the most basic and important truth about living in a community: you are protected, and you have obligations. Both parts matter. Both parts make life together possible.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The concept of a right** — understanding that certain protections belong to every person
2. **Bodily autonomy** — the right to say no to unwanted physical contact
3. **The right to speak and be heard** — the foundation of expression
4. **Property rights** — the right to keep what is yours and the responsibility to respect what is others'
5. **The paired nature of rights and responsibilities** — every right carries a corresponding duty
6. **The courage to speak up** — understanding that silence in the face of wrong is a choice
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Is a Right?

A right is something you are allowed to have or do that nobody can take away from you — not a bully, not a teacher, not even the government — without very good reason and a fair process.

Some rights are so important that they are written into the most important laws of the country. In the United States, many of these rights are in the **Bill of Rights** — the first ten amendments to the Constitution. You will study these in detail when you are older. For now, the important thing is to understand what a right feels like in your actual life.

**You have the right to be safe in your own body.** Nobody is allowed to hurt you. Nobody is allowed to touch you in a way you do not want. If someone does, you have the right to say "Stop," to move away, and to tell a trusted adult. Your body belongs to you. This is the most basic right there is.

**You have the right to speak.** You are allowed to say what you think, ask questions, disagree, and share your ideas. This does not mean you can say anything you want at any time — there are times when it is your turn to listen, and there are ways of speaking that are disrespectful. But the basic right to express your thoughts is yours.

**You have the right to be treated fairly.** Nobody should be treated worse than others because of how they look, where they come from, or what their family is like. Fairness does not mean everyone gets the same thing — it means everyone gets what they need and nobody is treated unjustly.

**You have the right to your own things.** What belongs to you is yours. Nobody can just take it. This is a property right, and it is important — it means your toys, your drawings, your books, your clothes are protected. Other people must ask before they use them.

### Part 2: Every Right Has a Partner

Here is the part that many people forget: every right comes with a responsibility. They are partners. You cannot have one without the other.

**The right to speak comes with the responsibility to listen.** If you demand that people hear you but refuse to hear them, you are not exercising a right — you are being a bully. Listening is the responsibility that makes the right to speak work for everyone.

**The right to be safe comes with the responsibility not to hurt others.** You have the right to be safe, and so does every other person. That means you cannot hit, push, or harm anyone else. Your right to safety and their right to safety are the same right — and both require you to respect it.

**The right to be treated fairly comes with the responsibility to treat others fairly.** You cannot demand fairness for yourself and treat others unfairly. It does not work that way. If fairness matters — and it does — it must matter for everyone, not just for you.

**The right to your own things comes with the responsibility to respect what belongs to others.** If you want people to leave your things alone, you must leave their things alone. If you want to borrow something, you ask. If something is not yours, you do not take it.

This is the core principle: **Rights and responsibilities go together.** A person who claims all the rights and none of the responsibilities has not understood what rights are. Rights are how we make life together possible. They only work when everyone — not just you, not just the other person, but everyone — takes both sides seriously.

### Part 3: When Something Is Wrong, You Can Speak Up

Sometimes you will see something that is not right. Someone is being bullied. Someone is being hurt. A rule is being broken. Something is unfair.

When that happens, you have a choice. You can stay quiet, or you can speak up. Both are choices, and this packet is honest about the fact that speaking up is harder.

Speaking up does not mean you have to be loud or aggressive. It means:
- Telling a trusted adult when someone is being hurt or when something is wrong
- Saying "That is not fair" when you see someone being treated unjustly
- Standing with someone who is being mistreated, even if it is uncomfortable
- Asking for help when you cannot fix the situation yourself

Speaking up takes courage. Some people call this being a "bystander" when you stay quiet and an "upstander" when you act. The words matter less than the reality: when something wrong is happening, the person who notices and does something about it is contributing to a better community. The person who notices and looks away is choosing to let the wrong continue.

You will not always know the perfect thing to say. That is fine. Saying something imperfect is almost always better than saying nothing.

### Part 4: Rules Protect Rights

Remember the rules for shared spaces from 4.5.3? Those rules exist because they protect rights. The rule that cars stop at red lights protects your right to cross the street safely. The rule against stealing protects your right to your own property. The rule that you cannot hit someone protects everyone's right to be safe.

Rules can sometimes feel annoying. Why do you have to wait your turn? Why can you not play your music as loud as you want? Why do you have to share the playground equipment? Because other people have rights too, and the rules are how everyone's rights are protected at the same time.

Not every rule is good. Sometimes rules are unfair. Sometimes rules protect some people but not others. When you are older, you will study what to do about unjust rules — and it is one of the hardest questions in civic life. For now, the important thing is to understand that rules exist for a reason, and the best reason is to protect everyone's rights equally.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Rights Articulation
**Developing well:** The child can name at least three rights and explain them in their own words. They understand rights as protections that belong to everyone.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot name specific rights, or treats rights as things only they have.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you discussed specific rights in specific situations? Abstract teaching does not work at this age. Concrete, situational examples do.

### 2. Paired Responsibility
**Developing well:** The child consistently identifies the responsibility that accompanies each right. They understand that rights without responsibilities do not work.

**Developing slowly:** The child focuses on rights as entitlements without acknowledging corresponding duties.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling paired thinking? "I have the right to rest, and I have the responsibility to make sure dinner is made first." Show the child what paired rights-responsibilities look like in adult life.

### 3. Speaking Up
**Developing well:** The child is willing to speak up when they see something wrong — to an adult, to a peer, or on behalf of someone else. They are developing the habit of moral courage.

**Developing slowly:** The child consistently stays silent when they witness unfairness or harm. They avoid conflict even when intervention is appropriate.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child afraid of consequences for speaking up? Have they been punished for speaking up in the past? Create a safe environment where speaking up is valued and supported. Some children need explicit permission: "If you see something wrong, I want you to tell me. You will not get in trouble for telling me."
